coredata.dc:description: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the most common malignancy-related cause of death worldwide. The prognosis of CRC patients is strongly correlated with elevated plasma copper. The quantitative detection of plasma copper is highly essential for the field of biomedicine, and our hypothesis was that plasma copper may be a therapeutic target for treating CRC. Here, we developed metformin-based carbon dots (MFCDs). MFCDs have strong photoluminescence at 460 nm, which is quenched by Cu2+. Cu2+ concentration and the fluorescence emission intensity of MFCDs are significantly associated (linear range of 0.5–40 μM with a detection limit of 0.30 μM). Additionally, administration of MFCDs significantly inhibited the growth of CRC xenografts in mice caused by the existence of biguanide functional groups. MFCDs treatment led to the increase of reactive oxygen species and subsequent DNA damage and cell cycle arrest. This study showed that the biguanide functional groups of MFCDs chelate with Cu2+, resulting in the reduction of Cu/Zn-superoxide dismutase and glutathione, which eventually reduces antioxidant capacity and ultimately induces cell apoptosis. These results indicate that MFCDs provide a novel approach for anticancer therapy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the most common malignancy-related cause of death worldwide. The prognosis of CRC patients is strongly correlated with elevated plasma copper. The quantitative detection of plasma copper is highly essential for the field of biomedicine, and our hypothesis was that plasma copper may be a therapeutic target for treating CRC. Here, we developed metformin-based carbon dots (MFCDs). MFCDs have strong photoluminescence at 460 nm, which is quenched by Cu2+. Cu2+ concentration and the fluorescence emission intensity of MFCDs are significantly associated (linear range of 0.5–40 μM with a detection limit of 0.30 μM). Additionally, administration of MFCDs significantly inhibited the growth of CRC xenografts in mice caused by the existence of biguanide functional groups. MFCDs treatment led to the increase of reactive oxygen species and subsequent DNA damage and cell cycle arrest. This study showed that the biguanide functional groups of MFCDs chelate with Cu2+, resulting in the reduction of Cu/Zn-superoxide dismutase and glutathione, which eventually reduces antioxidant capacity and ultimately induces cell apoptosis. These results indicate that MFCDs provide a novel approach for anticancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Metformin (MF) has been used worldwide for the therapy of diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and polycystic ovary syndrome [1]. Recently, the use of MF in the treatment of cancer has gotten more attention [2,3]. The incidence of colorectal cancer (CRC), one of the leading causes of death worldwide, has been steadily rising in a number of regions, including Eastern Europe, Southeast Asia, and South Asia [4,5]. According to a report, patients with diabetes who received MF treatment for a long time had a lower risk of developing CRC [6]. However, MF as an anti-tumor agent required ultra-high doses. For example, mice bearing prostate tumors and breast cancer cells required to be treated with 250 mg/kg/day and 2 mg/mL MF, respectively [7,8]. These does are higher than the recommended range for treating diabetes (8.3–33 mg/kg/day). The AMPK/PI3K/Akt signaling pathway is the main mechanism by which MF inhibits tumor cell growth and proliferation and induces apoptosis in tumor cells [9,10]. By interacting with the PEN2 protein, Ma et al. recently found that MF promoted the activation of AMPK to inhibit the activation of the lysosomal proton pump, v-ATPase [11]. Meanwhile, it has been shown that colorectal cancer patients have higher mean serum copper levels (165.99 vs. 98.84 mug/dl) (P < 0.001) [12,13]. CRC can be improved by lowering Cu levels [14]. By blocking the copper chaperone for superoxide dismutase 1 (CCS), which mediates the oxidative stress of Cu/Zn-Superoxide Dismutase (Cu/Zn-SOD) enzymes, copper chelators in cells prevent tissue damage and promote organ regeneration [15,16]. However, superoxide dismutase (SOD) oxidative stress has not been improved by copper chelator therapy in clinical settings. Tetrathiomolybdate (TTM), a copper chelator, was employed in one instance to treat conditions brought on by copper overload. Cu level reduction by a straightforward injection of Cu chelator was found to have serious adverse effects, including erythema, optic neuritis, vomiting, and cancer cytopenia, as well as slightly higher treatment costs [17–19]. Therefore, the creation of a novel CRC treatment is essential.

The signaling pathway has long been a focus the of attention in studies on the anti-tumor mechanism [20–22]. However, few studies have looked at the molecular structural basis for the anti-tumor properties of MF. The methyl and biguanide functional groups made up the MF molecular structure are what control its physicochemical properties. In this work, we synthesize metformin-based carbon dots (MFCDs), guanidinium phosphate-based carbon dots (GCDs), and dimethylguanidine-based carbon dots (DCDs) using polymerization to retain biguanidyl, guanidyl and methyl groups to synthesize, respectively, and explore the antitumor effects of functional groups. We apply carbon dots (CDs) because of their small size (less than 10 nm), chemical stability, excellent PL properties, outstanding bioimaging capability and low toxicity. In addition, it is possible to preserve the structural and functional characteristics of the precursor in the carbon dots by designing and controlling the chemical reaction of polymerization carbonization [23–28]. Although relevant metformin preparations of carbon dots have been reported [29–31], we investigated MF as a mechanism for treating CRC by maintaining particular functional groups inherited from MF precursors through carbon dots.

Here, we employ a hydrothermal method to create MFCDs while preserving the biguanide functional groups (BFG) of the raw material, MF, as a precursor. In the current study, we looked at whether MFCDs can chelate Cu2+, whether they can slow the growth of tumors, and, if so, what the molecular mechanism is. The MFCDs exhibit strong blue PL at around 460 nm without excitation-wavelength dependence and can chelate Cu2+. MFCDs meanwhile prevent tumor growth. On the one hand, low-dose MFCDs chelate Cu2+ through the BFG to reduce copper chaperones for superoxide dismutase (CCS)-mediated activation of copper-zinc-superoxide dismutase (Cu/Zn-SOD). On the other hand, they convert Cu2+ to Cu+, which promote the conversion of glutathione (GSH) into oxidized glutathione disulfide (GSSG). Decreases in antioxidant activity and the capacity to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) are brought on by drops in Cu/Zn-SOD and GSH levels. After DNA is damaged as a result of the subsequent rise in ROS, the cell cycle is stopped at the G2/M phase, which results in tumor cells dying (Scheme 1 ). CRC is generally treated with low-dose MFCDs and BFG. In the treatment of cancer, it overcomes the limitations of ultra-high-dose MF. Through the angle of molecular structure, this work provides insights on the anti-tumor mechanism of MFCDs, offering a new perspective on how anti-tumor medications might be created.

Metformin hydrochlorid, Guanidinium phosphate, dimethyl sulfoxide, and 2,2-dithiodibenzoic acid were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Acetic acid, N,N-dimethylformamied, and ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Commercially available SOD, NADPH/NADP+, GSH/GSSG, ROS, TUNEL and DFCH-DA kits were provided by Beyotime Biotechnology Company (Jiangsu, China). DMSO‑d 6 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and fetal calf serum (FBS) were obtained from Eβios Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). Deionized water was produced through a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore) and used throughout the study.

UV–vis absorption spectra were performed using a SHIMADZU UVmini-1240 UV-VIS spectrophotometer. PL spectra were measured using a VARIAN Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The XRD pattern was collected using a D8 ADVANCE (Brucker). The fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime are measured by the FLS1000/FS5. NMR measurements were taken by AVANCE III 600 M (Brucker). FT-IR spectra were taken on a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The TEM and HR-TEM images were recorded using a FEI, TECNAI TF20 field emission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using a Thermo ESCALAB 250 electronic spectrometer. Ultroviolet Photoelectron Spectrometer (UPS) was taken on a Thermo escalab 250Xi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the MFCDs and GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mmol Metformin hydrochloride and 1 mmol DTSA were dissolved into 10 mL acetic acid, then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon reactor and kept at 180 °C for 10 h in an air oven. After the hydrothermal treatment, the solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 20 min, and then the supernatant was taken. Afterward, the MFCDs solution was added into 500 mL boiled water to form MFCDs powder and wash out the residual raw materials and solvent. Finally, purified MFCDs powder was obtained through vacuum filtration.

GCDs are the same as the previous process for synthesis of MFCDs. 2 mmol Guanidinium phosphate and 1 mmol DTSA were dissolved into 10 mL acetic acid, then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon reactor and kept at 180 °C for 10 h in an air oven. After the hydrothermal treatment, the solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 20 min, and then the supernatant was taken. Afterward, the GCDs solution was added into 500 mL boiled water to form GCDs powder and wash out the residual raw materials and solvent. Finally, purified GCDs powder was obtained through vacuum filtration.

Cell viability and cell proliferation were assessed by MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (Sigma-Aldrich) assay. DU145, PC3, MIA-PaCa2, PANC1, Lovo, HCT116, SKOV3, OVCAR3 cells were obtained from The Cell Bank of Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Cells (1 × 104 cells/well) were seeded into 96-well plates. After treating cells with different concentrations of MFCDs for 24 h or 48 h, MTT solution in 1 × PBS was added to each well at the final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The plate was incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Then, the MTT formazan product was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and the optical density (OD) was measured at 490 nm wavelength on a multimode plate reader (infinite 200Pro, Tecan). The IC50 concentration obtained from the above experiments was used for cell proliferation experiments. MTT assay was also performed to measure cell growth under different MFCDs treatment groups, in this case, cells (0.3 × 104 cells/well) were seeded into 96-well plates. Each experiment was conducted in triplicates and repeated independently 3 times.

A cell suspension of 2 × 105 cells/100 μL was inoculated into a 6-well plate. After cells were fully apposed, cells were treated with different concentrations of MFCDs solution for 48 h. The suspended and adherent cells were collected, resuspended with 100 μL of assay buffer, double stained with 5 μL Annexin V and 5 μL PI (reagent manufacturer), incubated for 15 min at room temperature and protected from light, and terminated by adding 400 μL of 1 × binding buffer. The rate of apoptosis was measured by flow cytometry (FCM).

A cell suspension of 2 × 105 cells/100 μL was inoculated into a 6-well plate. After complete cell wall attachment, cells were treated with MFCDs solution at 50 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL for 48 h, respectively. Cells were collected by centrifugation and resuspended in pre-cooled 75% ethanol and fixed overnight at 4 °C. The next day, the cells were centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 10 min and the supernatant was discarded. The cells were resuspended in PBS and centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 10 min at room temperature and the supernatant was discarded. Add 500 μL of PI staining solution to the obtained samples and stain for 30 min at room temperature, taking care to incubate away from light. After termination of staining, the cells were centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 5 min. The supernatant was discarded and the cells were resuspended with 500 μL PBS. The cell cycle was detected using flow cytometry.

Several antibodies were used to assess the antigen expression on different cells types and have been indicated throughout the methods. A summary is indicated in the following list: Anti-CCS antibody (Affinity Biosciences Cat# DF3971, RRID: AB_2836324), β-actin antibody (Proteintech Cat# 66,009–1), ATP7A (Affinity Biosciences Cat# DF8506), P-ERK1/2 (Affinity Biosciences Cat# AF1015), ERK1/2 (Affinity Biosciences Cat# AF0155).

Cells (1 × 105 cells) were inoculated in 12-well plates with glass slide and incubated with 100 ng/μL concentration of MFCDs solution at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 4 h. After two times wash with PBS to remove the non-uptake MFCDs, the cells were fixed by 4% PFA and imaged by a ZEISS LSM880 with fast air scan laser scanning confocal microscope at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, Blue: λex = 405 nm and λem = 462 nm; Green: λex = 488 nm and λem = 562 nm; Blue: λex = 543 nm and λem = 614 nm.

100 μL of 0 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, and 25 μg/mL MFCDs had been injected subcutaneously at the tumor site. The fluorescence scans were performed before and after the injection. A small animal imaging system was used to acquire signals from 450 to 500 with a 400 nm laser as the excitation source by IVIS Lumina (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the tumor-bearing mouse model
ce:section.ce:para:
Female mice (BALB/c-nu, four weeks old, SPF grade) and forage (SPF grade) were purchased from Liaoning Changsheng biotechnology co., Ltd. The animal experiments performed in this study were approved by the Experimental Animal Welfare Ethics Committee of Harbin Institute of Technology and were by the legal requirements for experimental animals. The ethical approval code is IACUC-2021059 (Sept. 7, 2021). All experimental protocols were performed under the relevant guidelines, including any relevant details. The tumor model was constructed by subcutaneous injection of 2 × 106 Lovo cells into the right flank of each mouse. When tumors were allowed to grow for 7 d or when tumor size reached 100 mm3, 12 female BALB/c-nu tumor-bearing mice (16.0–20.0 g) were randomly divided into 3 groups. Three groups of mice were intratumorally injected with MFCDs-1 (0 mg/kg), MFCDs-2 (12.5 mg/kg), MFCDs-3 (25.0 mg/kg), every two days for 7 days. The mice were executed by cardiac blood sampling and blood was collected for biochemical analysis, while lung, heart, spleen, liver, kidney, and tumor tissues were taken for subsequent histopathological examination and metabolic analysis of MFCDs. The tumor volume (mm3) was obtained following equation (1):
V = l e n g t h ×
w i d t h
2
/ 2
.

Three groups of tumor tissues, MFCDs-1 (0 mg/kg), MFCDs-2 (12.5 mg/kg) and MFCDs-3 (25 mg/kg), were taken and sequentially fixed, dehydrated, transparent, dipped in wax and paraffin-embedded. Paraffin-embedded tumor specimens were cut into 4 μm slices, stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E), photographed, and observed under a light microscope. Histopathological examinations of the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys are performed similarly to those performed on tumors.

Blood from mice on the 7th day of treatment was taken for biochemical analysis. Hematological parameters were analyzed and tested by a hematology analyzer (ABAXIS-VetScan VS2, USA). Blood, lung, heart, spleen, liver, kidney, and tumor tissues of mice treated with MFCDs were taken and treated with chloroazide (HNO3/HCl = 1/3, V/V) at 70 °C for 24 h. The content of MFCDs with Cu2+ ions in the samples was determined by ICP-OES.

The representative data were expressed as mean ± SEM (n = 3). The photoacoustic data and IC50 were analyzed by GraphPad Prism 7 software. Group differences were performed using a two-tailed t-test or two-way ANOVA. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 and ****P < 0.0001 were considered statistically significant.

MFCDs were synthesized from MF hydrochloride and 2,2′-dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA)/acetic acid solution by a one-pot hydrothermal method (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The process of MFCDs synthesis is shown in Scheme 1. The MFCDs had an average diameter of about 2.3 nm with a narrow distribution (Fig. 1 a). Their small (nanometer-scale) size made it easier for them to enter cells and perform biomedical functions. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of the MFCDs showed a lattice-plane spacing of 0.2 nm corresponding to the graphite (100) plane; this indicated the formation of graphene quantum dots. The X-ray diffraction pattern of the MFCDs had a distinct diffraction peak at 25°, which was attributed to the (002) plane (Fig. 1b). In addition, there was a diffraction peak near 43°, corresponding to the (100) plane [32]. These results demonstrated the presence of many highly carbonized graphitic structures in the centers of the MFCDs. We also used X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to characterize the surface motifs of the MFCDs (Fig. S5A-E, Supporting Information) and found the presence of C, N, O, and S elements. The CN (286.85 eV) [33] and C–N (283.27 eV) [34] in the C 1s band, together with the N–H (399.75 eV) [35] and CN (395.89 eV) [36] in the N 1s band, demonstrated the possible existence of BFG-based structures (Fig. S5F, Supporting Information) [37]. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy confirmed that C–N (peaks at 1258, 1149, and 1037 cm−1) [38] and CN (peak at 1586 cm−1) [39] were connected to guanidine groups (Fig. S6, Supporting Information) [40]. These results implied that MFCDs were performed in DMSO solution due to the lack of hydrophilic groups consequently subsequent testing of MFCDs.

We used 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) to distinguish between sp3-hybridized and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (Fig. 1c–d). In the 1H NMR spectrum, a BFG signal was detected at 2–3 ppm, which originated from the adjacent BFG structure of metformin. An aromatic ring–ring signal was detected at 6.5–8.5 ppm, which was attributed to the proton resonance of the graphitized nuclei inside the MFCDs. The chemical shifts for N–CH3 (1.93 ppm) and N–H (12–14 ppm) implied that there were no individually exposed –NH3 groups on the surfaces of MFCDs. In the 13C NMR spectrum, signals in the range of 20–35 ppm were associated with aliphatic (sp3) carbons, and signals in the range of 100–180 ppm were associated with sp2 carbons. The signal in the 180–220 ppm range was consistent with that of the 1H NMR spectrum, showing a structure without carboxyl/amide groups. Under solvothermal conditions, a dehydration process occurred between DTSA and MF, forming a nanoscale graphite-like core and a surface with guanidinium groups.

Unlike most CDs, which had the characteristic of emission wavelength dependence [36], the MFCDs had strong fluorescence emission with a wavelength of around 465 nm, which was independent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 a). The absolute quantum yield of the MFCDs is 17.44% and the life time is 6.99 ns, as deduced from the integrating sphere of FL intensity (Fig. S3, Fig. S4 and Table S1, Supporting Information). The MFCDs solution looked light yellow–green and blue under daylight and ultraviolet (UV)-365 nm irradiation in the upright (Fig. 2b). The UV–visible light (UV–vis) absorption spectrum for the prepared MFCDs had peaks at
λ 1
≈ 310
n m
and
λ 2
≈ 350
n m
, which were attributed to
π −
π *
conversion of CC in the core of the MFCDs [41]. There was also a dominant band at
λ 3
≈ 417
n m
, which was attributed to
n −
π *
transition of the surface states containing CN/CO, C–O, and C–S structures [42]. The dependence of the MFCDs’ PL intensity on the irradiation time under UV-365 (365 nm, 1 mW) laser irradiation. As the irradiation time increased, the PL intensity of the MFCDs decreased gradually (Fig. 2c). The PL intensity had a near linear relationship with the irradiation time, with a linear correlation coefficient of
R 2
= 0.947
(Fig. 2d) and decreased about 34%. As irradiation continued, the intensities of the absorption peaks also decreased (Fig. 2e). The absorption intensities at
A 1
≈ 310
n m
and
A 2
≈ 417
n m
decreased linearly with light exposure time (
R 1 2
= 0.995 ,
R 2 2
= 0.978
) and decreased by about 12% and 36% after 120 min of irradiation, respectively (Fig. 2f). These results suggest that prolonged light irradiation leads to a reduction in
n −
π *
transitions in the CN/CO, C–O, and C–S structures on the surfaces of MFCDs. In order to utilize their emission characteristic in biological imaging applications, we co-cultured MFCDs with several types of cancer cells, including Lovo, PC3, SKOV3, HCT116, and MIA-PaCa2 cells, and observed the entry of MFCDs into the cells using confocal laser scanning microscopy (Fig. 2g). It can be clearly seen that MFCDs successfully reached the interior of the cells and maintained their fluorescence emission properties within the cytoplasm. These results represent that MFCDs can enter the cell interior, so in the future MFCDs can be designed as drug carriers or overlaid with other luminescent groups to synthesize new fluorescent probes [43]. The results lay a foundation for the further functionalization of MFCDs for applications in biomedicine.

We examined the sensitivity to the MFCDs of various tumor cell types, including pancreatic (MIA-PaCa2 and PANC1), colorectal (Lovo and HCT116), prostate (SKOV3 and OVCAR3), and ovarian (DU145 and PC3) cell lines. We found that Lovo cells were the most sensitive to MFCDs, showing significant proliferation inhibition (Fig. S7-8 and Table. S2, Supporting Information). The MFCDs showed more significant inhibition on the proliferation of Lovo cells compared with HCT116 cells, and a lower half-maximal inhibitory concentration (24 h: 73.28 vs. 63.99 μg/mL; 48 h: 10.07 vs. 58.87 μg/mL) (Fig. 3 a–c). Therefore, we chose Lovo cells for further study to elucidate the anti-tumor mechanism of the MFCDs. A tumor-bearing nude mouse model was established by subcutaneous injection of Lovo cells into BALB/c mice to evaluate the anti-tumor properties of the MFCDs (Fig. 3d). The MFCDs had no effect on mouse body weight but significantly reduced tumor volume and number in a concentration-dependent manner. Compared with the control group, one in four tumors completely disappeared in both the 12.5 mg/kg and the 25.0 mg/kg MFCD treatment groups (Fig. 3e–h). Pathological examinations showed that tumors exhibited increased fibrosis accompanied by increased cell apoptosis in the 25.0 mg/kg MFCDs treatment group (Fig. 3i–j). These results show that the MFCDs exert excellent anti-tumor effects by promoting apoptosis of tumor cells.

To evaluate the biosafety of the MFCDs, we performed histological and blood biochemical analyses. First, we examined histological changes of the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys using hematoxylin and eosin staining (Fig. S9, Supporting Information). The aggregation of multiple inflammatory cells with Kupffer cell proliferation was found in liver tissue of tumor-bearing mice. MFCDs treatment caused no marked pathological changes in these organs compared with those of control mice. The results of the biochemical analysis of blood samples from each group of mice (Table S3, Supporting Information). The levels of ALT and BUN in the control group were abnormal. After 25.0 mg/kg MFCDs treatment, all biochemical parameters were within the normal range, indicating no damage to the liver or kidney. All of the above data strongly suggest that the MFCDs are safe for in vivo applications and are effective against CRC.

We performed fluorescence quenching experiments on MFCDs in different metal ions, amino acids and FBS solutions. The results showed that MFCDs could achieve efficient fluorescence quenching by chelating Cu2+ (Fig. S10-12, Supporting Information). After junctional convolution of the XPS data of Cu 2p, Cu+ and Cu2+ accounted for 53.54% and 46.46% of the Cu 2p ratio, respectively. Meanwhile, the XPS spectrum of MFCDs shifted towards high binding energy overall after exposure to copper ions, among which the N 1s fine spectrum was the most obvious. It is possible that the nitrogen-containing structure plays a role in the process of chelating copper ions (Fig. S13 and Table S4, Supporting Information). This was accompanied by partial conversion of Cu2+ to Cu+ in the process of chelation of MFCDs and Cu2+, and is accompanied by the formation of Cu–O [44,45] (Fig. 4 a). At Cu2+ concentrations of 0–500 μM, the fluorescence quenching intensity gradually increased with increasing Cu2+ concentration and reached saturation at a quenching ratio of 50% (Fig. 4b). The quenching intensity of MFCDs’ PL emission showed a strong linear relationship with Cu2+ in a range of 0.5–40 μM (Fig. 4c). The linear regression equation was
Y = 0.01235 X + 0.001109
(
R 2
= 0.99013
)
. The working limit of detection (LOD) was 0.30 μM, as calculated by the formula LOD = 3S/K, where S is the standard deviation of the signal (n = 3) and K is the slope of the linear calibration plot. By comparing the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of Cu2+ ions to the MFCDs solution, it was found that the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched by the addition of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 4d). On the other hand, the addition of ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) to the MFCDs solution showed the same significant enhancement. However, the fluorescence intensity failed to recover completely after the addition of Cu2+ ions first and then EDTA was added after sufficient mixing. These results suggest that at that time, after MFCDs were well mixed with Cu2+ ions, the biguanide group chelated with Cu2+ ions and the fluorescence emission center was reduced leading to the occurrence of fluorescence quenching behavior. Meanwhile, with the addition of EDTA, EDTA only made part of Cu2+ ions detach from MFCDs, so that the fluorescence intensity of group-2 was partially recovered. These results imply that MFCDs have equal chelating ability for Cu2+ ions compared to EDTA [46]. Based on the structural characteristics of MF and DTSA and combined with the previously reported work [42], we inferred the reaction process in the preparation of MFCDs, the BFG was exposed just enough to be involved in the subsequent treatment of CRC (Fig. 4e). To further confirm that MFCDs with BFG were capable of chelating Cu2+, we synthesized guanidinium phosphate-based CDs (GCDs) that did not contain the BFG structure.

In the 1H NMR experiments, we found that when MF coexisted with Cu2+, the paramagnetic contribution of Cu(II) to the spin-lattice relaxation (R1) rate of the H proton caused Cu@MF to broaden and weaken the signal at the BFG structure (2–3 ppm) (Fig. 4f), suggesting that the BFG in MF forms a complex structure with copper. Similarly, the 1H NMR results demonstrated that the MFCDs formed a complex structure with copper (Fig. 4g). However, after the chelation of Cu2+, the signal of MFCDs became weaker, the peak area was wider, and the overall signal level of Cu@MFCDs was smaller than that of MFCDs. The GCDs showed no fluorescence quenching behavior towards Cu2+, and no BFG structures were found (Fig. 4h–j and Fig. S14, Supporting Information). Moreover, no copper signal was found in the Cu 2p fine spectrum of Cu@GCDs after the addition of Cu2+ (Fig. 4k and Fig. S15, Table S5, Supporting Information), and the 1H NMR signal of the GCDs did not weaken (Fig. 4l). These results demonstrate that GCDs with guanidyl characteristics cannot chelate Cu2+. On the other hand, we used dimethylguanidine-based CDs (DCDs) obtained by the same preparation process using 1,1-dimethylguanidine sulfate to exclude the effect of the dimethyl structure. The DCDs did not exhibit any weakening of the 1H NMR signal (Fig. 4m). These results demonstrate that the dimethyl structure is not related to copper chelation. In addition, we synthesized another five CDs using substances of biguanidyl or guanidyl as precursors. The CDs prepared with BFG could chelate copper, whereas those prepared with guanidyl could not (data not shown). These results demonstrate that the chelation of Cu2+ by MFCDs originates from the BFG structure on the surface. This creates a foundation for the further development of Cu2+-chelating MFCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCDs exert their anti-tumor effect by reducing antioxidant capacity
ce:section.ce:para: We examined the contents of Cu2+ in blood, liver, kidney, and tumor tissues of tumor-bearing mice. Significant reductions in serum and tumor copper levels were found after MFCDs treatment (Fig. 5 a); this demonstrated that the MFCDs could retain their copper-chelating properties in living organisms and could therefore be used in biomedical research. Next, we compared the effects of MFCDs with those of copper chelator tetrathiomolybdate (TTM) at a cellular level. MFCDs were found to have a similar role to that of TTM in the presence of exogenous copper. CCS expression decreased after chelation of Cu2+ by MFCDs in Lovo cells (Fig. 5b and Fig. S16, Supporting Information). Therefore, we detected the total SOD and Cu/Zn-SOD contents after MFCDs treatment. Compared with the control group, we found that total SOD and Cu/Zn-SOD were significantly decreased in the MFCDs and TTM treatment groups. However, the total SOD and Cu/Zn-SOD contents did not show any change in the group treated with GCDs (which did not contain the BFG structure) (Fig. 5c–d). Compared with GCD and TTM, MFCDs reduced the contents of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) and the oxidized form of nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) (Fig. 5e–f). These results show that the BFG of MFCDs bonded with Cu2+. MFCDs inhibited the CCS-mediated activation of Cu/Zn-SOD, resulting in decreased antioxidant capacity.

Then, we analyzed the GSH levels of tumor-bearing mice. The ratio of GSH to GSSG in the control group was ∼72.47%, decreasing to ∼9.46% and ∼13.74% after treatment with different concentrations of MFCDs, respectively (Fig. 5g). These results suggest that MFCDs reduce the antioxidant capacity of tumors. Subsequently, we found that ROS levels were significantly increased in the MFCDs treatment group compared with the control group (Fig. 5h and Fig. S17, Supporting Information). With increasing concentration of MFCDs, excess ROS resulted in cell arrest at G2/M phase. At 100 μg/mL, about 37.3% of Lovo cells were blocked in the G2/M phase. However, 100 μg/mL GCDs had little effect on the cell cycle of Lovo cells (Fig. 5i). After Annexin V-APC/PI double-staining, apoptotic cells were detected using flow cytometry. There was significant apoptosis in the MFCDs treatment group but almost no apoptotic cells in the GCDs treatment group (Fig. 5j). Meanwhile, it was lower in the GCDs treated group (100 μg/mL: Q1+Q2
≈
39.34%) than in the MFCDs treated group (100 μg/mL: Q1+Q2
≈ 66.10%). Meanwhile, MFCDs were more potent for killing Lovo cells in the additional copper ion environment. It is implied that MFCDs will play a greater role when CRC symptoms are aggravated (Fig. S18, Supporting Information). These results indicate that after MFCDs treatment, ROS levels exceeded a critical value and promoted tumor cell apoptosis, consistent with the result of the TUNEL assay (Fig. 3j).

The above experimental results tentatively identify the anti-tumor mechanism of MFCDs. Namely, MFCDs bind Cu2+ via BFG, reducing CCS-mediated activation of Cu/Zn-SOD. At the same time, GSH is depleted owing to the shift of the copper valence state during the binding process, which results in decreased antioxidant capacity and reduced ability to eliminate ROS. Excess ROS causes DNA damage, arrests cells in the G2/M phase, and contributes to the apoptosis of tumor cells.

MF, a traditional diabetes drug, has recently been the subject of unexpected results in several anti-tumor studies. However, the MF dosages required to treat tumors are much higher than the suggested clinical dosages. We created MFCDs using a hydrothermal process and MF as the precursor. The precursor BFG, which can chelate Cu2+ to achieve sensitive detection of 0.5–40 μM, is also kept by the MFCDs. In some studies, high copper concentrations were discovered in tumor tissues and cancer patients' serum [47]. Moreover, colon cancer patients with poor healing have high serum copper levels [48]. Although copper chelators have been used to reduce copper levels as an adjunct to cancer treatment, the cause of elevated copper levels is unknown and can be associated with side effects [49]. In order to reduce copper levels and have anti-tumor effects, we discovered that the surface-specific BFG of MFCDs could chelate with Cu2+. In our work, it was shown that the maximum therapeutic dose of MFCDs (25 mg/kg) had no negative effects on the mouse's organs, in contrast to the liver and kidney damage brought on by TTM overdose [50]. These findings suggest that MFCDs for colon cancer treatment have excellent biosafety.

Copper-dependent activation of the mitogen-activated kinase pathway is a significant factor in tumor growth, and copper stimulates MEK1 and ERK1/2 phosphorylation through interaction with Cu-MEK1 [51]. Targeting copper is proving to be a viable strategy to limit the growth of cancer. Additionally, the regulatory impact of the copper chaperone protein CCS was substantial. This research found that MFCDs did not significantly alter the Cu transporter ATP7A after chelating copper and were unable to activate ERK1/2 (Fig. S12, Supporting Information). Both the non-enzymatic system (like GSH) and the enzymatic system (like SOD) serve as antioxidants. CCS is used to transport intracellular Cu2+ is to Cu/Zn-SOD [52,53]. CCS promotes the formation of disulfide bonds while also activating Cu/Zn-SOD and having an antioxidant effect by introducing Cu2+ to Cu/Zn-SOD through protein-protein interactions [54]. Through view of this, we looked into the structural foundations of the anti-tumor mechanism of MFCDs. By binding of Cu2+ by BFG, MFCDs prevented CCS from activating Cu/Zn-SOD, which reduced antioxidant capacity and triggered apoptosis.

The previous structural findings indicate that the Cu2+ to Cu+ conversion occurs during the copper chelation process by MFCDs. Equation (1) (the conversion of GSH to GSSG) was also a part of this process [55]. Then, using the Fenton reaction, Cu+ reacted with hydrogen peroxide to produce
∙ O H
and Cu2+ (equation (2)) [56]. We discovered that the GSH/GSSG ratio fell following MFCD therapy.
(1)
C u
2 +
+ G S H →
C u
+
+ G S S G
(2)
C u
+
+
H 2
O 2
→
C u
2 +
+ ∙ O H +
O H
−

GSH as a significant antioxidant. DNA damage is caused by GSH depletion and
∙ O H
synthesis [55]. Because GSH scavenges ROS, its downregulation has a direct impact on ROS levels in vivo [57]. MFCDs reduced GSH while converting Cu2+ to Cu+ during copper chelation. Cu2+ was chelated by MFCDs in order to prevent STEAP family metalloreductases from reducing Cu2+ to Cu+, which indirectly reduced free Cu+ levels and decreased Cu/Zn-SOD activity [58]. Cu/Zn-SOD and GSH levels decreased, which had an effect on their ability to scavenge ROS and have antioxidant properties. Normally, cells have a level on how much ROS they can tolerate. Beyond this point, DNA damage, cell arrest in the G2/M phase, and tumor cell apoptosis occurs as a result of the ROS system going out of balance [47,59]. Additionally, GSH depletion raises ROS levels, and it has been demonstrated that blocking the G2/M phase makes tumor cells more susceptible to radiotherapy (RT) [60–63].

In conclusion, we created fluorescent MFCDs with low toxicity and stable optical properties. The MFCDs reduced CCS-mediated activation of Cu/Zn-SOD by chelating copper through the BFG. Furthermore, they decreased GSH to raise ROS levels and damage DNA. The fact that Lovo cells exposed to the MFCDs experienced apoptosis due to a G2/M phase arrest suggests that they may one day be used for both radiosensitization and antitumor therapy. The MFCDs' imaging capabilities and ability to inhibit CRC when exposed to high copper levels make them suitable for use in multimodal and multifunctional diagnosis and therapy. In the area of nanomaterial synthesis and applications, our work provided a new way for the application of the traditional drug MF and offers a platform the for the biomedical.
